title: Chargés d'affaires

infobox.value: (French pronunciation: [[ʃaʁʒe dafɛʁ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q150_(fra)-Arthur_Crbz-charg%C3%A9_d%E2%80%99affaires.wav "File:LL-Q150 (fra)-Arthur Crbz-chargé d’affaires.wav")), plural ***chargés d'affaires***, often shortened to ***chargé*** (French) and sometimes in colloquial English to ***charge-D***, is a [diplomat](/wiki/Diplomat "Diplomat") who serves as an embassy's [chief of mission](/wiki/Chief_of_mission "Chief of mission") in the absence of the [ambassador](/wiki/Ambassador "Ambassador"). The term is [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") for "person charged with business", meaning they are responsible for the duties of an ambassador. *Chargé* is masculine in [gender](/wiki/Grammatical_gender "Grammatical gender"); the feminine form is ***chargée d'affaires*** (pronounced the same way).

outgoing_links.target_title: Permanent representative to the United Nations

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Chargés d'affaires](/w/index.php?title=Charg%C3%A9s_d%27affaires&redirect=no "Chargés d'affaires"))

Head of diplomatic mission when no higher official exists

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Charge_crop.jpg/250px-Charge_crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:Charge_crop.jpg)

[Persia](/wiki/Qajar_dynasty "Qajar dynasty")'s *chargé d'affaires* and [his wife](/wiki/Florence_Breed_Khan "Florence Breed Khan") visiting U.S. President [Woodrow Wilson](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson "Woodrow Wilson") at the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House") in May 1913

| Diplomats |
| --- |
| * [Ambassador](/wiki/Ambassador "Ambassador") * [High commissioner](/wiki/High_commissioner "High commissioner") * [Permanent representative](/wiki/Permanent_representative "Permanent representative") * [Nuncio](/wiki/Nuncio "Nuncio") * [Head of mission](/wiki/Head_of_mission "Head of mission") * [Plenipotentiary](/wiki/Plenipotentiary "Plenipotentiary") * [Envoy](/wiki/Envoy_(title) "Envoy (title)") * [Deputy chief of mission](/wiki/Deputy_chief_of_mission "Deputy chief of mission") * [Minister](/wiki/Minister_(diplomacy) "Minister (diplomacy)") * [Resident minister](/wiki/Resident_minister "Resident minister") * [Papal legate](/wiki/Papal_legate "Papal legate") * Chargé d'affaires * [Consul](/wiki/Consul_(representative) "Consul (representative)") * [Attaché](/wiki/Attach%C3%A9 "Attaché") * [Military attaché](/wiki/Military_attach%C3%A9 "Military attaché") * [Cultural attaché](/wiki/Cultural_attach%C3%A9 "Cultural attaché") * [Agricultural attaché](/wiki/Agricultural_attach%C3%A9 "Agricultural attaché") * [Science attaché](/wiki/Science_attach%C3%A9 "Science attaché") * [Diplomatic courier](/wiki/Diplomatic_courier "Diplomatic courier") |
| * [v](/wiki/Template:Diplomats "Template:Diplomats") * [t](/wiki/Template_talk:Diplomats "Template talk:Diplomats") * [e](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Diplomats "Special:EditPage/Template:Diplomats") |

A **chargé d'affaires** (French pronunciation: [[ʃaʁʒe dafɛʁ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q150_(fra)-Arthur_Crbz-charg%C3%A9_d%E2%80%99affaires.wav "File:LL-Q150 (fra)-Arthur Crbz-chargé d’affaires.wav")), plural ***chargés d'affaires***, often shortened to ***chargé*** (French) and sometimes in colloquial English to ***charge-D***, is a [diplomat](/wiki/Diplomat "Diplomat") who serves as an embassy's [chief of mission](/wiki/Chief_of_mission "Chief of mission") in the absence of the [ambassador](/wiki/Ambassador "Ambassador"). The term is [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") for "person charged with business", meaning they are responsible for the duties of an ambassador. *Chargé* is masculine in [gender](/wiki/Grammatical_gender "Grammatical gender"); the feminine form is ***chargée d'affaires*** (pronounced the same way).

A *chargé* enjoys the same privileges and immunities as an ambassador under [international law](/wiki/Vienna_Convention_on_Diplomatic_Relations "Vienna Convention on Diplomatic Relations"), and normally these extend to their aides as well.[[1]](#cite_note-1) However, *chargés d'affaires* are [outranked](/wiki/Diplomatic_rank "Diplomatic rank") by ambassadors and have lower precedence at formal diplomatic events.[[2]](#cite_note-state_precedence-2) In most cases, a diplomat serves as a *chargé d'affaires* on a temporary basis in the absence of the ambassador. In unusual situations, in cases where disputes between the two countries make it impossible or undesirable to send agents of a higher [diplomatic rank](/wiki/Diplomatic_rank "Diplomatic rank"), a *chargé d'affaires* may be appointed for an indefinite period.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChisholm1911-3)

## Types of *chargés*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Charg%C3%A9_d%27affaires&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Types of chargés")]

*Chargés d'affaires* *ad interim* ("a. i.") are those who temporarily head a diplomatic mission in the absence of the accredited head of that mission. It is usual to appoint a [minister-counsellor](/wiki/Minister-counsellor "Minister-counsellor"), counsellor, or embassy secretary as *chargé d'affaires* *ad interim*, and that person is presented to the foreign minister of the receiving state.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChisholm1911-3) By definition, *chargés d'affaires* *ad interim* do not possess [diplomatic credentials](/wiki/Letter_of_credence "Letter of credence").[[4]](#cite_note-boczek2005-4)

*Chargés d'affaires* *en pied* ("e. p.") are appointed to be permanent heads of mission, in cases where the two countries lack ambassadorial-level relations.[[5]](#cite_note-us_venezuela-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) They are appointed by [letters of credence](/wiki/Letter_of_credence "Letter of credence") from the foreign minister of the sending state to the foreign minister of the receiving state. *Chargés d'affaires* *en pied* have [precedence](/wiki/Diplomatic_rank "Diplomatic rank") over *chargés d'affaires* *ad interim*, but are outranked by ambassadors. They are sometimes referred to as *chargés d'affaires* *[ad hoc](/wiki/Ad_hoc "Ad hoc")* or *en titre*.[[2]](#cite_note-state_precedence-2)[[7]](#cite_note-freeman1993-7)[[4]](#cite_note-boczek2005-4)

## Long-term *chargé*-level relations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Charg%C3%A9_d%27affaires&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Long-term chargé-level relations")]

In certain cases, a *chargé d'affaires* may be appointed for long periods, such as when a mission is headed by a non-resident ambassador who is accredited to multiple countries. In addition, a mission may be downgraded from an ambassadorial to a *chargé d'affaires* level to show displeasure, yet avoid taking the extremely serious step of breaking diplomatic relations. For example, [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia "Saudi Arabia") and [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand") did not exchange ambassadors between 1989 and 2022,[[8]](#cite_note-8) due to the [Blue Diamond Affair](/wiki/Blue_Diamond_Affair "Blue Diamond Affair").[[9]](#cite_note-9) The [Holy See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See") recalled the ambassador to [Taipei](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan") right after [United Nations General Assembly Resolution 2758](/wiki/United_Nations_General_Assembly_Resolution_2758 "United Nations General Assembly Resolution 2758") ceased to recognize the Republic of China as the sole legitimate representative of China, and thus *chargé d'affaires* becomes the most top-ranked de facto envoy to [Taipei](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan") since then (the ambassador did not leave his position until 1979).

Sometimes when diplomatic recognition is extended to a new government, a *chargé* may be sent to immediately establish diplomatic representation. However, if a timely exchange of ambassadors does not take place, this may result in a prolonged period of *chargé*-level relations. For example, the United Kingdom recognized the [People's Republic of China](/wiki/People%27s_Republic_of_China "People's Republic of China") in 1950 and posted a *chargé d'affaires* in the new capital of [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing"). However, China was unwilling to exchange ambassadors until the United Kingdom withdrew its consulate from [Taipei](/wiki/Taipei "Taipei"). [Sino-British relations](/wiki/China%E2%80%93United_Kingdom_relations "China–United Kingdom relations") were not upgraded to the ambassadorial level until 1972.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Since a *chargé d'affaires* presents his or her [credentials](/wiki/Letter_of_credence "Letter of credence") to the foreign minister rather than the head of state, the appointment of a *chargé* may avoid a politically sensitive meeting that would imply approval or recognition of that head of state or government. Similarly, the receiving country may decline to receive an ambassador, but still maintain diplomatic relations by accepting a *chargé*. For example, the [Republic of Cyprus](/wiki/Republic_of_Cyprus "Republic of Cyprus") appoints a number of *chargés d'affaires* *en pied* to its embassies abroad.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Charg%C3%A9_d%27affaires&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]

In modern use, *chargés d'affaires* essentially differ from [ambassadors](/wiki/Ambassador_(diplomacy) "Ambassador (diplomacy)") in that, like all diplomats, *chargés* represent their nation, but unlike an ambassador, they are not personal representatives of their head of state. Apart from [rank and precedence](/wiki/Diplomatic_rank "Diplomatic rank"), *chargés* enjoy the same privileges and immunities as other diplomatic agents.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChisholm1911-3)

However, there have been rare historical circumstances in which the title *chargé d'affaires* was in fact employed in a more significant colonial role, as commonly held by a [resident](/wiki/Resident_(title) "Resident (title)"). Thus, in [Annam](/wiki/Annam_(French_protectorate) "Annam (French protectorate)")-[Tonkin](/wiki/Tonkin_(French_protectorate) "Tonkin (French protectorate)") (most of present [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam")), the first French *chargé d'affaires* at [Huế](/wiki/Hu%E1%BA%BF "Huế"), the local ruler's capital, since 1875, one of them (three terms) was appointed the first [resident-general](/wiki/Resident-general "Resident-general") on 11 June 1884, as they stopped being tributary to the Chinese Empire, less than a year after the 25 August 1883 French protectorates over Annam and Tonkin (central and northern regions).[*[further explanation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

In French, *chargé d'affaires* may be used outside diplomacy to designate an individual with some more or less temporary responsibility for a specific area of business, understood broadly.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Spelling and grammar

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Charg%C3%A9_d%27affaires&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Spelling and grammar")]

*Chargé d'affaires* generally follows French usage: *chargé d'affaires* is singular, *chargés d'affaires* for plural. The *"d'affaires"* is always in the plural form, and should be lowercase even if *Chargé* is capitalized. Following the French declension, *chargée d'affaires* (with the feminine ending) may be seen where the *chargé* is female.

For temporary *chargés*, *ad interim* may or may not be added depending on the context, but is always lower case; it may be italicized or shortened to simply a.i.

Retrieved 20 March 2016.

4. ^ [***a***](#cite_ref-boczek2005_4-0) [***b***](#cite_ref-boczek2005_4-1) Boczek, Boleslaw Adam (2005). *International Law: A Dictionary*. Scarecrow Press. p. 48. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0810850781](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0810850781 "Special:BookSources/978-0810850781").
5. **[^](#cite_ref-us_venezuela_5-0)** ["U.S. Relations With Venezuela"](https://2009-2017.state.gov/r/pa/ei/bgn/35766.htm). *Burean of Western Hemisphere Affairs, U.S. Department of State*. July 20, 2015. "There currently is no U.S. Ambassador to Venezuela; the U.S. Charge d'Affaires en pied is Lee McClenny."
6. **[^](#cite_ref-6)** Denza, Eileen (2016). *Diplomatic Law: Commentary on the Vienna Convention on Diplomatic Relations*. Oxford University Press. p. 99. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [9780198703969](/wiki/Special:BookSources/9780198703969 "Special:BookSources/9780198703969"). "Chargés d'affaires *ad interim* are distinct from the 'chargés d'affaires accredited to Ministers for Foreign Affairs' who are listed under Article 14 of the Convention as forming the third class of heads of mission. The latter are often described as 'chargés d'affairs *en pied* ', and can be traced back to the eighteenth century 'agents'."
7. **[^](#cite_ref-freeman1993_7-0)** Freeman, Charles W. Jr. (1993). *Diplomat's dictionary*. Washington, DC: National Defense University Press. p. 56. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [9780788125669](/wiki/Special:BookSources/9780788125669 "Special:BookSources/9780788125669"). "Chargé d'Affaires: An officer in charge of an embassy who is not an ambassador, e.g., when the level of relations between two states has been lowered to below the ambassadorial level, and accredited to the Minister of Foreign Affairs, rather than to the chief of state. In such circumstances, termed *Chargé d'Affaires ad hoc.*"
8. **[^](#cite_ref-8)** Vérité, Clément (26 January 2022). ["Saudi Arabia and Thailand restore ambassadors after three decades of tensions"](https://www.newsendip.com/saudi-arabia-and-thailand-restore-relations-after-three-decades-of-tensions-blue-diamond/). *Newsendip*.
9. **[^](#cite_ref-9)** ["Embassy issues another angry statement"](https://web.archive.org/web/20100922105214/http://www.nationmultimedia.com/home/2010/09/19/national/Embassy-issues-another-angry-statement-30138286.html). *nationmultimedia.com*. Archived from [the original](http://www.nationmultimedia.com/home/2010/09/19/national/Embassy-issues-another-angry-statement-30138286.html) on 2010-09-22. Retrieved 2013-03-12. "Mr. Nabil Ashri said that "The Kingdom of Saudi Arabia's goal on this issue stems directly from its firm and clear foreign policy of non-interference, and seeking closer cooperation and developing friendly international relations, as its relations with the Kingdom of Thailand suffered because of the appalling unsolved crimes currently pending and which have resulted in the downgrading of bilateral relations affecting both the people of Saudi Arabia and Thailand for more than 20 years", adding "it is my duty to try to negotiate this issue with the Thai Government, in order to avoid any further tension or undesirable complications in bilateral relations"."
10. **[^](#cite_ref-10)** ["China (Exchange of Ambassadors)"](https://api.parliament.uk/historic-hansard/commons/1972/mar/13/china-exchange-of-ambassadors). *[Parliamentary Debates (Hansard)](/wiki/Hansard "Hansard")*. House of Commons. 13 March 1972. col. 31–35. "When early last year we decided to resume discussions with the Chinese Government on this subject we told them that if an agreement on an exchange of ambassadors was reached we would be prepared to withdraw our consulate from Taiwan. This we shall now do."
11. **[^](#cite_ref-11)** ["Cyprus Diplomatic Missions Abroad"](https://web.archive.org/web/20160425230019/http://www.mfa.gov.cy/mfa/mfa2006.nsf/protocol04_en/protocol04_en?OpenDocument). Archived from [the original](http://www.mfa.gov.cy/mfa/mfa2006.nsf/protocol04_en/protocol04_en?OpenDocument) on 25 April 2016. Retrieved 19 May 2013.
